speaker: Guest lecturer (former Eagar student/colleague, metallurgist; not Tom Eagar)
speaker_notes: The speaker refers to Tom in the third person as 'Professor Eagar' and notes that Tom asked him to give this talk. The speaker has a francophone accent. He worked at Bombardier Aerospace and was on the I-35W Minneapolis bridge collapse investigation team. He teaches a fall class that elaborates on safety factor methodology.
topics: factor of safety vs probability of failure
high-speed boat prop shaft fatigue failure
stainless steel pressure vessel stress corrosion cracking
Washington DC steam piping drip leg failure
Gaussian variability in material strength and load
coefficient of variation and factor of ignorance
cases_referenced: New Orleans canal levee failure (Katrina, design SF 1.3)
I-35W Minneapolis bridge collapse (2007, gusset plate)
Florida high-speed boat prop shaft (400-hr design life)
Stainless steel food-processing tank (tomato paste, chloride SCC)
Washington DC government building steam piping drip leg
BP / Macondo / Deepwater Horizon blowout preventer
figures_referenced: Safety factor ranges: 1.2 (boat prop), 1.3 (NOLA levee), 1.5–1.6 (aircraft), 2 (buildings), 3 (automotive), 4 (pressure vessels), 8 (some specialized designs)
Bridge probability of failure: ~5 × 10⁻⁶ per year
I-35W bridge: ~40 years of service before 2007 collapse
Helicopter blade spar: 30,000 cycles at 12 KSI / 18 KSI design example
Grade 8 bolt: 150 KSI tensile, ~20 KSI in tension-compression fatigue (factor of ~7 reduction)
Bolt preload reduces stress amplitude by factor of ~6–7
